Model performance on ToolBeHonest inversely correlates with diagnostic depth, indicating increased struggle with deeper reasoning (Level-3 missing-tool analysis) and complex scenarios (Limited Functionality Tools).

Larger model parameters do not consistently translate to better performance; factors like training token count and context window (e.g., Llama-3 vs. Llama-2) play a more critical role, alongside models' response strategies.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for tool-augmented Large Language Models primarily evaluate tool utilization under ideal, solvable conditions, assuming complete and functional toolsets.
There was a lack of comprehensive diagnostic benchmarks specifically designed to assess and understand hallucination in tool-augmented LLMs when tasks are unsolvable or toolsets are problematic.

Models often generate incorrect or unsupported content (hallucinate) when confronted with tasks that cannot be solved with the given tools, posing reliability and safety risks in real-world applications.
results: ToolBeHonest proved challenging for all tested models; Gemini-1.5-Pro achieved the highest overall score of 45.3, with GPT-4o scoring 37.0 and the best open-weight model (Llama-3-70B) scoring 14.6.
All models struggled significantly with Level-3 (missing-tool analysis) and in Limited Functionality Tools (LFT) scenarios, indicating difficulty in deep analysis of tool limitations.

Solvability hallucination was the most common error, particularly in Level-3 tasks, alongside frequent instances of predicting non-existent or wrong tools; models performed dramatically worse on unsolvable tasks (39.4% of proprietary model performance) compared to solvable ones (91.1%).
solution: ToolBeHonest introduces a multi-level diagnostic benchmark to assess hallucination in tool-augmented LLMs, focusing on scenarios where tasks are unsolvable or toolsets are problematic.

It employs a three-level diagnostic framework: Level-1 for solvability detection (macro-level), Level-2 for solution planning (micro-level), and Level-3 for detailed missing-tool analysis and rationale.

The benchmark includes three hallucination-inducing scenarios: Missing Necessary Tools (MNT), Potential Tools (PT) which tempt models to use unlisted tools, and Limited Functionality Tools (LFT) where provided tools have limitations.
paper_summary.summary: ToolBeHonest is a multi-level diagnostic benchmark designed to assess and understand hallucination in tool-augmented Large Language Models when faced with unsolvable tasks or problematic toolsets. The benchmark reveals that current LLMs, especially open-weight models, struggle significantly with recognizing task unsolvability and articulating reasons for missing functionalities, leading to frequent hallucination.

title: ToolBeHonest: A Multi-level Hallucination Diagnostic Benchmark for Tool-Augmented Large Language Models

abstract: Existing multimodal browsing benchmarks often fail to require genuine multimodal reasoning, as many tasks can be solved with text-only heuristics without vision-in-the-loop verification. We introduce MMSearch-Plus, a 311-task benchmark that enforces multimodal understanding by requiring extraction and propagation of fine-grained visual cues through iterative image-text retrieval and cross-validation under retrieval noise. Our curation procedure seeds questions whose answers require extrapolating from spatial cues and temporal traces to out-of-image facts such as events, dates, and venues. Beyond the dataset, we provide a model-agnostic agent framework with standard browsing tools and a set-of-mark (SoM) module, which lets the agent place marks, crop subregions, and launch targeted image/text searches. SoM enables provenance-aware zoom-and-retrieve and improves robustness in multi-step reasoning. We evaluated closed- and open-source MLLMs in this framework. The strongest system achieves an end-to-end accuracy of 36.0%, and integrating SoM produces consistent gains in multiple settings, with improvements up to +3.9 points. From failure analysis, we observe recurring errors in locating relevant webpages and distinguishing between visually similar events. These results underscore the challenges of real-world multimodal search and establish MMSearch-Plus as a rigorous benchmark for advancing agentic MLLMs.
title: MMSearch-Plus: Benchmarking Provenance-Aware Search for Multimodal Browsing Agents

abstract: As LLM-based agents are increasingly deployed in real-life scenarios, existing benchmarks fail to capture their inherent complexity of handling extensive information, leveraging diverse resources, and managing dynamic user interactions. To address this gap, we introduce VitaBench, a challenging benchmark that evaluates agents on versatile interactive tasks grounded in real-world settings. Drawing from daily applications in food delivery, in-store consumption, and online travel services, VitaBench presents agents with the most complex life-serving simulation environment to date, comprising 66 tools. Through a framework that eliminates domain-specific policies, we enable flexible composition of these scenarios and tools, yielding 100 cross-scenario tasks (main results) and 300 single-scenario tasks. Each task is derived from multiple real user requests and requires agents to reason across temporal and spatial dimensions, utilize complex tool sets, proactively clarify ambiguous instructions, and track shifting user intent throughout multi-turn conversations. Moreover, we propose a rubric-based sliding window evaluator, enabling robust assessment of diverse solution pathways in complex environments and stochastic interactions. Our comprehensive evaluation reveals that even the most advanced models achieve only 30% success rate on cross-scenario tasks, and less than 50% success rate on others. Overall, we believe VitaBench will serve as a valuable resource for advancing the development of AI agents in practical real-world applications. The code, dataset, and leaderboard are available at this https URL

keyInsights: Agentic task complexity in real-world applications can be formally measured across three dimensions: reasoning, tool use, and dynamic interaction.
Graph-based tool dependency modeling enables autonomous agent behavior and cross-domain reasoning, moving beyond rigid policy adherence.
Robust evaluation of complex, long-horizon, multi-turn trajectories requires a rubric-based sliding window approach that persistently tracks criterion satisfaction.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent benchmarks operate in simplified, controlled settings, failing to capture the intricate complexities of real-world deployments.
Current evaluation frameworks inadequately consider dynamic user interactions, diverse external resources, and extensive environmental information.
Many benchmarks impose rigid, domain-specific policies, limiting agents' autonomous exploration and reasoning capabilities in dynamic environments.
results: State-of-the-art LLM agents achieve only a 30.0% average success rate on cross-scenario tasks, indicating significant limitations in complex real-world application contexts.
"Thinking" mechanisms (e.g., chain-of-thought) consistently enhance agent effectiveness and efficiency, leading to higher success rates and fewer interaction turns.
Detailed error analysis reveals that reasoning errors account for the majority (61.8%) of failures in complex tasks, followed by tool usage errors (21.1%) and interaction errors (7.9%).
solution: VitaBench features 400 interactive tasks directly derived from real "life-serving applications" (delivery, in-store, online travel) to evaluate agents across reasoning, tool, and interaction complexity.
Tool use is modeled as a graph structure to inherently encode domain rules, enabling autonomous agent exploration across interconnected action spaces without verbose policy documents.
A sophisticated user simulator introduces realistic, dynamic multi-turn interaction challenges through unique profiles, behavioral attributes, and progressive information disclosure.
paper_summary.summary: VitaBench, developed by the Meituan LongCat Team, introduces a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on versatile interactive tasks derived from real-world "life-serving applications." It assesses agents across reasoning, tool use, and interaction complexity, revealing that even state-of-the-art models achieve only a 30.0% average success rate on cross-scenario tasks.

title: VitaBench: Benchmarking LLM Agents with Versatile Interactive Tasks in Real-world Applications

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are evolving from text generators into reasoning agents. This transition makes their ability to use external tools a critical capability. However, evaluating this skill presents a significant challenge. Existing benchmarks are often limited by their reliance on synthetic tools and severely constrained action spaces. To address these limitations, we introduce MCPVerse, an expansive, real-world benchmark for evaluating agentic tool use. MCPVerse integrates more than 550 real-world, executable tools to create an unprecedented action space exceeding 140k tokens, and employs outcome-based evaluation with real-time ground truth for time-sensitive tasks. We benchmarked the state-of-the-art LLMs across three modes (Oracle, Standard, and Max-Scale), revealing that while most models suffer performance degradation when confronted with larger tool sets, the agentic models, such as Claude-4-Sonnet, can effectively leverage expanded exploration spaces to improve accuracy. This finding not only exposes the limitations of state-of-the-art models in complex, real-world scenarios but also establishes MCPVerse as a critical benchmark for measuring and advancing agentic tool use capabilities.
title: MCPVerse: An Expansive, Real-World Benchmark for Agentic Tool Use

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) hold promise for accelerating scientific discovery by interpreting complex experimental procedures. However, their true capabilities are poorly understood, as existing benchmarks neglect the fine-grained and long-horizon nature of authentic laboratory work, especially in wet-lab settings. To bridge this gap, we introduce ExpVid, the first benchmark designed to systematically evaluate MLLMs on scientific experiment videos. Curated from peer-reviewed video publications, ExpVid features a new three-level task hierarchy that mirrors the scientific process: (1) Fine-grained Perception of tools, materials, and actions; (2) Procedural Understanding of step order and completeness; and (3) Scientific Reasoning that connects the full experiment to its published conclusions. Our vision-centric annotation pipeline, combining automated generation with multi-disciplinary expert validation, ensures that tasks require visual grounding. We evaluate 19 leading MLLMs on ExpVid and find that while they excel at coarse-grained recognition, they struggle with disambiguating fine details, tracking state changes over time, and linking experimental procedures to scientific outcomes. Our results reveal a notable performance gap between proprietary and open-source models, particularly in high-order reasoning. ExpVid not only provides a diagnostic tool but also charts a roadmap for developing MLLMs capable of becoming trustworthy partners in scientific experimentation.

keyInsights: Proprietary MLLMs like GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5 consistently outperform open-source models and human baselines, especially in higher-order procedural understanding and scientific reasoning.

Current MLLMs struggle with fine detail disambiguation, tracking subtle state changes over time, and robustly linking visual experimental procedures to their broader scientific outcomes and discoveries.

While scaling improves performance in open-source models, 'thinking' strategies (e.g., chain-of-thought) do not consistently enhance video-grounded reasoning and can sometimes lead to reliance on logical priors rather than visual evidence.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) lack systematic evaluation on complex, real-world scientific experiment videos, especially for fine-grained and long-horizon tasks.
Existing video understanding benchmarks primarily focus on general human actions or outcome-focused medical scenarios, failing to address the unique challenges of 'wet-lab' scientific procedures.
Current knowledge-driven and scientific benchmarks often rely on static images or text, neglecting the temporal and visual complexities inherent in dynamic experimental videos.
A critical gap exists in systematically evaluating MLLMs' ability to perceive experimental manipulations, check procedural fidelity, and connect observations to scientific conclusions.
results: GPT-5 achieved the highest average score in Level-3 scientific reasoning tasks at 56.4%, with the best open-source model scoring only 39.6%, indicating a substantial performance gap.
Gemini-2.5-Flash (with 'think') led Level-1 perception tasks with 60.2% accuracy, surpassing the human baseline of 37.6% and the best open-source InternVL3-78B at 50.9%.

Ablation studies confirmed that visual inputs are indispensable, with performance significantly degrading (near zero for Level-3 tasks) without frames, while some models effectively leveraged up to 256 frames for improved accuracy.
solution: The team curated EXPVID using step-by-step experiment videos from JoVE, accompanied by ASR transcripts and peer-reviewed papers, ensuring high-quality visual and textual content.

They designed a three-level hierarchical task structure to evaluate MLLMs: fine-grained perception (Level-1), procedural understanding (Level-2), and scientific reasoning (Level-3) with 10 distinct tasks.
A vision-centric annotation pipeline, combining LLM assistance with rigorous multi-disciplinary expert validation, was employed to generate over 7,800 high-quality, vision-dependent QA pairs.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai AI Laboratory, Institute of Science Tokyo, and Nanjing University developed EXPVID, the first benchmark for scientific experiment video understanding and reasoning, leveraging JoVE videos and peer-reviewed papers. The benchmark evaluates Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) across perception, procedural understanding, and scientific reasoning tasks, revealing that proprietary models like GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5 significantly outperform open-source counterparts in complex scientific contexts.

title: ExpVid: A Benchmark for Experiment Video Understanding & Reasoning

abstract: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have ushered in a new era in artificial intelligence, merging capabilities in both language and vision to form highly capable Visual Foundation Agents. These agents are postulated to excel across a myriad of tasks, potentially approaching general artificial intelligence. However, existing benchmarks fail to sufficiently challenge or showcase the full potential of LMMs in complex, real-world environments. To address this gap, we introduce VisualAgentBench (VAB), a comprehensive and pioneering benchmark specifically designed to train and evaluate LMMs as visual foundation agents across diverse scenarios, including Embodied, Graphical User Interface, and Visual Design, with tasks formulated to probe the depth of LMMs' understanding and interaction capabilities. Through rigorous testing across nine proprietary LMM APIs and eight open models, we demonstrate the considerable yet still developing agent capabilities of these models. Additionally, VAB constructs a trajectory training set constructed through hybrid methods including Program-based Solvers, LMM Agent Bootstrapping, and Human Demonstrations, promoting substantial performance improvements in LMMs through behavior cloning. Our work not only aims to benchmark existing models but also provides a solid foundation for future development into visual foundation agents. Code, train \& test data, and part of fine-tuned open LMMs are available at \url{this https URL}.

keyInsights: Even advanced proprietary large multimodal models (LMMs) struggle on the benchmark, achieving low overall success rates.
A substantial performance gap persists between proprietary and fine-tuned open-source LMMs.
Models demonstrate uneven capabilities across different visual domains, excelling in some while struggling in others, and visual grounding significantly improves performance.
originalProblem: Existing LMM benchmarks primarily evaluate visual language capabilities or focus on single environments, limiting assessment of general-purpose visual agent abilities.
Current evaluations often rely on offline assessments rather than interactive testing, which is crucial for agent capabilities.
There is a lack of comprehensive multi-domain benchmarks and sufficient training trajectories to develop and evaluate LMMs as generalist visual foundation agents.
results: GPT-4o, the best-performing proprietary model, achieved an average success rate of 36.2% across all VisualAgentBench environments.
Fine-tuning improved open-source LMM performance, with InternVL2 achieving a 16.0% success rate, though still 20.2 percentage points lower than GPT-4o.
The presence of object labels in visual input substantially improved model performance in embodied and GUI environments, highlighting the importance of visual grounding.
solution: Introduces VisualAgentBench (VAB), a multi-domain interactive benchmark with five datasets across embodied (OmniGibson, Minecraft), GUI (Mobile, WebArena-Lite), and visual design (CSS) environments.
Evaluates LMMs as vision-centric agents capable of high-level decision-making through direct interaction with environments via a standardized interface.
Provides 14,000 high-quality training trajectories collected via program-based solvers, LMM bootstrapping, and human demonstrations to enable fine-tuning of open-source models.
paper_summary.summary: VisualAgentBench (VAB) is presented as a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate large multimodal models (LMMs) as interactive visual foundation agents across diverse embodied, GUI, and visual design environments. The evaluation reveals that even leading models achieve modest success rates and demonstrates the importance of visual grounding and error recovery for such agents.

title: VisualAgentBench: Towards Large Multimodal Models as Visual Foundation Agents

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly serving as autonomous agents, and their utilization of external tools via the Model Context Protocol (MCP) is considered a future trend. Current MCP evaluation sets suffer from issues such as reliance on external MCP services and a lack of difficulty awareness. To address these limitations, we propose MCPAgentBench, a benchmark based on real-world MCP definitions designed to evaluate the tool-use capabilities of agents. We construct a dataset containing authentic tasks and simulated MCP tools. The evaluation employs a dynamic sandbox environment that presents agents with candidate tool lists containing distractors, thereby testing their tool selection and discrimination abilities. Furthermore, we introduce comprehensive metrics to measure both task completion rates and execution efficiency. Experiments conducted on various latest mainstream Large Language Models reveal significant performance differences in handling complex, multi-step tool invocations. All code is open-source at Github.

keyInsights: Many LLM agents prioritize task completion over execution efficiency, leading to a substantial decline (over 10 points) in Task Efficiency Finish Score (TEFS) compared to Task Finish Score (TFS).
LLM agents, particularly certain OpenAI models, demonstrate widespread deficiencies in efficiently executing parallel tool calls, with some recording zero TEFS for dual parallel tasks.
Agent performance generally degrades as task complexity increases (more tool invocations) and when more distractor tools are present in the candidate list.
originalProblem: Existing MCP tool-use benchmarks suffer from instability and lack reproducibility due to reliance on remote, live MCP servers.
Current benchmarks offer insufficient granularity in task difficulty assessment and often lack metrics for evaluating execution efficiency.
Evaluation frameworks do not systematically test LLM agents' robustness in selecting correct tools when presented with distractors.
results: Claude Sonnet 4.5 exhibited strong performance across TFS, TEFS, and Time Efficiency, often leveraging an aggressive parallelization strategy.
Most LLM agents showed a consistent drop of over 10 points in TEFS compared to TFS, indicating a trade-off between task completion and efficient execution.
OpenAI models demonstrated an "extreme serial approach," leading to zero TEFS scores for Dual Parallel tasks, highlighting a significant challenge in handling parallel tool invocations.
solution: Developed an automated evaluation framework using Autogen with local MCP server deployment, ensuring stable and reproducible testing.
Curated a dataset of 178 authentic tasks and thousands of MCP tools, utilizing LLM-assisted annotation and manual curation.

Introduced a dynamic sandbox environment to test agent robustness with distractor tools and defined new metrics for task completion, execution efficiency (TEFS), token efficiency, and time efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: Peking University researchers developed MCPAgentBench, a new benchmark for evaluating large language model (LLM) agents' use of Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools, providing a stable, reproducible evaluation environment with local server deployment. It introduces metrics for task efficiency and agent robustness to distractors, revealing a consistent performance drop of over 10 points for most models when strict efficiency (TEFS) is considered compared to mere task completion (TFS).

title: MCPAgentBench: A Real-world Task Benchmark for Evaluating LLM Agent MCP Tool Use

abstract: Recent advancements extend Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) beyond standard visual question answering to utilizing external tools for advanced visual tasks. Despite this progress, precisely executing and effectively composing diverse tools for complex tasks remain persistent bottleneck. Constrained by sparse tool-sets and simple tool-use trajectories, existing benchmarks fail to capture complex and diverse tool interactions, falling short in evaluating model performance under practical, real-world conditions. To bridge this gap, we introduce VisualToolChain-Bench(VTC-Bench), a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate tool-use proficiency in MLLMs. To align with realistic computer vision pipelines, our framework features 32 diverse OpenCV-based visual operations. This rich tool-set enables extensive combinations, allowing VTC-Bench to rigorously assess multi-tool composition and long-horizon, multi-step plan execution. For precise evaluation, we provide 680 curated problems structured across a nine-category cognitive hierarchy, each with ground-truth execution trajectories. Extensive experiments on 19 leading MLLMs reveal critical limitations in current models' visual agentic capabilities. Specifically, models struggle to adapt to diverse tool-sets and generalize to unseen operations, with the leading model Gemini-3.0-Pro only achieving 51% on our benchmark. Furthermore, multi-tool composition remains a persistent challenge. When facing complex tasks, models struggle to formulate efficient execution plans, relying heavily on a narrow, suboptimal subset of familiar functions rather than selecting the optimal tools. By identifying these fundamental challenges, VTC-Bench establishes a rigorous baseline to guide the development of more generalized visual agentic models.

keyInsights: Current MLLMs exhibit a universal ceiling in active, multi-step tool orchestration, with model scaling alone not resolving bottlenecks, as even top models barely exceed 50% accuracy.

Proprietary models generally establish stronger baselines and show substantial performance improvements with tool augmentation compared to open-source models, which often yield limited gains or even degradation.

Models frequently resort to suboptimal trial-and-error heuristics and over-rely on a narrow subset of basic tools, exposing a critical deficit in precise programmatic orchestration and compositional reasoning.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for agentic MLLMs rely on sparse tool-sets and simple tool invocations, failing to assess multi-tool composition and long-horizon planning required for complex visual reasoning.
Current evaluation frameworks often focus solely on final predictions, obscuring underlying logical reasoning, planning abilities, and tool execution precision of MLLMs.
A lack of diverse and multi-tool compositional challenges in benchmarks hinders the identification of true operational limits and the guidance for developing more versatile visual agents.
results: The top-performing model, Gemini-3.0-Pro, achieved an overall score of 51.18% with tool augmentation, while most other MLLMs clustered around 30% accuracy, underscoring the benchmark's difficulty.

Models demonstrated significant inefficiencies in tool utilization, with GPT-5.2 recording a Mean Absolute Error of 9.96 in attempted calls and a Tool Usage Efficiency of only 16.78%, indicating high redundancy and suboptimal execution.

Performance improved with increased prompt comprehensiveness and the provision of ground-truth tools, but overall gains remained bounded, suggesting fundamental limitations in synthesizing correct multi-step execution logic.
solution: VTC-Bench curates 32 distinct visual operations from the OpenCV library, organized into four functional modules (Geometry, Enhancement, Feature Extraction, Drawing) to support complex visual tasks.
It comprises 680 meticulously designed problems across a nine-category cognitive hierarchy, requiring multi-step tool orchestration and long-horizon planning.

The benchmark provides ground-truth execution trajectories and employs metrics like Average Pass Rate (APR), Tool Call Rate (TCR), and Tool Usage Efficiency (Eff_tool) for precise diagnostic analysis.
paper_summary.summary: VTC-Bench is introduced as a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating multimodal large language models' (MLLMs) proficiency in compositional visual tool chaining, featuring 32 distinct OpenCV-based visual operations and 680 multi-step problems. Evaluations on 19 leading MLLMs reveal a universal performance ceiling, with the top model reaching 51.18% accuracy, indicating current models face significant challenges in complex multi-step tool orchestration.

title: VTC-Bench: Evaluating Agentic Multimodal Models via Compositional Visual Tool Chaining

abstract: The rapid advancement of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has enabled browsing agents to acquire and reason over multimodal information in the real world. But existing benchmarks suffer from two limitations: insufficient evaluation of visual reasoning ability and the neglect of native visual information of web pages in the reasoning chains. To address these challenges, we introduce a new benchmark for visual-native search, VisBrowse-Bench. It contains 169 VQA instances covering multiple domains and evaluates the models' visual reasoning capabilities during the search process through multimodal evidence cross-validation via text-image retrieval and joint reasoning. These data were constructed by human experts using a multi-stage pipeline and underwent rigorous manual verification. We additionally propose an agent workflow that can effectively drive the browsing agent to actively collect and reason over visual information during the search process. We comprehensively evaluated both open-source and closed-source models in this workflow. Experimental results show that even the best-performing model, Claude-4.6-Opus only achieves an accuracy of 47.6%, while the proprietary Deep Research model, o3-deep-research only achieves an accuracy of 41.1%. The code and data can be accessed at: this https URL
title: VisBrowse-Bench: Benchmarking Visual-Native Search for Multimodal Browsing Agents

abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs), equipped with increasingly advanced planning and tool-use capabilities, are evolving into autonomous agents capable of performing multimodal web browsing and deep search in open-world environments. However, existing benchmarks for multimodal browsing remain limited in task complexity, evidence accessibility, and evaluation granularity, hindering comprehensive and reproducible assessments of deep search capabilities. To address these limitations, we introduce BrowseComp-$V^3$, a novel benchmark consisting of 300 carefully curated and challenging questions spanning diverse domains. The benchmark emphasizes deep, multi-level, and cross-modal multi-hop reasoning, where critical evidence is interleaved across textual and visual modalities within and across web pages. All supporting evidence is strictly required to be publicly searchable, ensuring fairness and reproducibility. Beyond final-answer accuracy, we incorporate an expert-validated, subgoal-driven process evaluation mechanism that enables fine-grained analysis of intermediate reasoning behaviors and systematic characterization of capability boundaries. In addition, we propose OmniSeeker, a unified multimodal browsing agent framework integrating diverse web search and visual perception tools. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that even state-of-the-art models achieve only 36% accuracy on our benchmark, revealing critical bottlenecks in multimodal information integration and fine-grained perception. Our results highlight a fundamental gap between current model capabilities and robust multimodal deep search in real-world settings.

keyInsights: Current state-of-the-art MLLMs exhibit a significant performance gap compared to human participants in multimodal deep search tasks, indicating the challenging nature of the benchmark.
External tool augmentation is critical for MLLMs to handle dynamic, cross-modal evidence chains on the open web, as internal parametric knowledge alone is insufficient for these tasks.

Process-level evaluation reveals that MLLMs frequently succeed at individual sub-goals but struggle with maintaining logical consistency and integrating information across long sequences to reach correct final answers.
Visual grounding, image perception, and long-horizon planning are identified as primary bottlenecks for MLLMs in complex web browsing environments, with perception errors being dominant.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for multimodal browsing agents often lack sufficient task complexity, typically limited to shallow information retrieval and concentrating visual information in initial stages.
Key information in prior benchmarks is frequently inaccessible or not publicly searchable, hindering reproducibility and fair evaluation of models.
Previous evaluations predominantly focus on final answer accuracy, offering limited insight into an agent's reasoning process, planning capabilities, or specific failure modes.
results: Human participants achieved an average Success Rate (SR) of 68.03% and a Process Score (PS) of 82.93%, while no evaluated MLLM surpassed 40% SR, with GPT-5.2-Thinking reaching 39.13%.

Tool-free MLLMs showed significantly lower performance (around 10% SR), whereas tool-augmented models, especially with the OmniSeeker framework, achieved substantial improvements, such as GPT-5.2 reaching 36.00% SR.

Model performance consistently declined as task complexity increased and search depth grew, with 'Visual Grounding Error' and 'Image Perception Failure' identified as the dominant error categories across all models.
solution: BrowseComp-V3 comprises 300 complex questions requiring deep, multi-level, and cross-modal multi-hop reasoning, spanning diverse domains and designed to prevent reliance on parametric knowledge alone.
The benchmark ensures all critical evidence is publicly accessible via standard web search engines and provides manually annotated gold-standard search trajectories for reproducibility.
An expert-validated, subgoal-driven process evaluation mechanism is implemented to allow for fine-grained characterization of search behaviors and diagnostic analysis of model capabilities.
The OmniSeeker framework is provided, integrating standardized tools for web search, content retrieval, and image processing, to facilitate comprehensive and unified evaluation of various MLLMs.
paper_summary.summary: BrowseComp-V3 is a multimodal browsing benchmark featuring 300 complex, hand-crafted questions that necessitate deep, multi-level, and cross-modal reasoning, alongside a process-oriented evaluation. Experiments with state-of-the-art MLLMs on this benchmark show a substantial performance gap compared to humans and reveal critical bottlenecks in visual perception and long-horizon planning.

title: BrowseComp-$V^3$: A Visual, Vertical, and Verifiable Benchmark for Multimodal Browsing Agents

abstract:
Multimodal Large Language models (MLLMs) have shown promise in web-related
tasks, but evaluating their performance in the web domain remains a challenge
due to the lack of comprehensive benchmarks. Existing benchmarks are either
designed for general multimodal tasks, failing to capture the unique
characteristics of web pages, or focus on end-to-end web agent tasks, unable to
measure fine-grained abilities such as OCR, understanding, and grounding. In
this paper, we introduce \bench{}, a multimodal benchmark designed to assess
the capabilities of MLLMs across a variety of web tasks. \bench{} consists of
seven tasks, and comprises 1.5K human-curated instances from 139 real websites,
covering 87 sub-domains. We evaluate 14 open-source MLLMs, Gemini Pro, Claude-3
series, and GPT-4V(ision) on \bench{}, revealing significant challenges and
performance gaps. Further analysis highlights the limitations of current MLLMs,
including inadequate grounding in text-rich environments and subpar performance
with low-resolution image inputs. We believe \bench{} will serve as a valuable
resource for the research community and contribute to the creation of more
powerful and versatile MLLMs for web-related applications.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Current state-of-the-art MLLMs, including proprietary models, still face substantial challenges in accurately understanding and grounding information within complex web pages.
Performance on VisualWebBench shows limited correlation with scores on general MLLM benchmarks (MMMU) and end-to-end web agent benchmarks (Mind2Web), indicating unique web-specific challenges.
MLLMs' ability to process higher input image resolutions is crucial for web comprehension, particularly for discerning small text and intricate UI elements.
originalProblem: Existing general MLLM benchmarks (e.g., VQA) do not adequately evaluate understanding of web-specific characteristics like complex layouts, small text, and interactive elements.
Web agent benchmarks (e.g., Mind2Web) focus on end-to-end task completion, overlooking the fine-grained perceptual, understanding, and grounding abilities critical for web interaction.
There was no comprehensive benchmark to systematically assess MLLMs' capacity to understand and ground information on real-world web pages, hindering targeted model improvements.
results: Proprietary models like Claude Sonnet (65.8%) and GPT-4V (64.6%) achieved the highest average scores, significantly outperforming open-source MLLMs, with LLaVA-1.6-34B reaching 50.5%.
Model scaling, as observed in the LLaVA-1.6 series (7B to 34B), consistently improved performance across most web understanding tasks.

GUI-specialized MLLMs (SeeClick, CogAgent) did not show overall performance gains on VisualWebBench tasks, with SeeClick even underperforming its base model, while excelling in direct bounding box grounding where general MLLMs struggled.
solution: Introduces VisualWebBench, a novel, comprehensive multimodal benchmark comprising 1.5K human-curated instances from 139 real-world websites across diverse domains.
Designed with a multi-granular approach (website, element, action levels) and seven distinct QA-style tasks to probe specific MLLM capabilities in web page understanding and grounding.
Evaluates MLLMs using a combination of metrics like ROUGE-L, SQuAD F1, and Accuracy for tasks ranging from captioning and OCR to element grounding and action prediction.
paper_summary.summary: VisualWebBench introduces a comprehensive multimodal benchmark to assess MLLMs' understanding and grounding capabilities within real-world web pages. Evaluations reveal that even state-of-the-art MLLMs find web-specific tasks challenging, with proprietary models like GPT-4V and Claude Sonnet achieving around 65% accuracy, significantly outperforming open-source models and highlighting critical areas for improvement in web-aware AI.

title:
VisualWebBench: How Far Have Multimodal LLMs Evolved in Web Page
  Understanding and Grounding?

abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) evolve from passive text generators to active reasoning agents capable of interacting with external tools, the Model Context Protocol (MCP) has emerged as a key standardized framework for dynamic tool discovery and orchestration. Despite its widespread industry adoption, existing evaluation methods do not adequately assess tool utilization capabilities under this new paradigm. To address this gap, this paper introduces MCP-RADAR, the first comprehensive benchmark specifically designed to evaluate LLM performance within the MCP framework. MCP-RADAR features a challenging dataset of 507 tasks spanning six domains: mathematical reasoning, web search, email, calendar, file management, and terminal operations. It quantifies performance based on two primary criteria: answer correctness and operational accuracy. To closely emulate real-world usage, our evaluation employs both authentic MCP tools and high-fidelity simulations of official tools. Unlike traditional benchmarks that rely on subjective human evaluation or binary success metrics, MCP-RADAR adopts objective, quantifiable measurements across multiple task domains, including computational resource efficiency and the number of successful tool-invocation rounds. Our evaluation of leading closed-source and open-source LLMs reveals distinct capability profiles and highlights a significant trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. Our findings provide actionable insights for both LLM developers and tool creators, establishing a standardized methodology applicable to the broader LLM agent ecosystem. All implementations, configurations, and datasets are publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: A significant disparity exists between an LLM's ability to syntactically execute tool calls (high DTSR) and its failure to select the semantically appropriate tool (low RA) for solving a problem, pointing to superficial understanding.
The primary challenge for current LLMs is not determining if a tool is needed, but rather which specific tool to invoke and how to parameterize it correctly for the task at hand.
There is a clear trade-off between performance and efficiency, with some open-source models achieving competitive accuracy at the cost of significantly higher computational resource consumption.
originalProblem: Existing LLM evaluation methods are insufficient for comprehensively assessing tool utilization within the Model Context Protocol (MCP) paradigm, which is widely adopted in the industry.

Current tool-centric benchmarks often conflate different abilities, rely on purely simulated environments, have limited scope for complex multi-turn or multi-tool scenarios, and use restrictive binary metrics.
Many datasets for tool-use evaluation are limited to operational-matching tasks or are entirely synthetic, lacking real-world relevance and fidelity.
results: Closed-source models generally demonstrated superior performance over open-source models, particularly in mathematical reasoning, with Gemini-2.5-Pro emerging as a top performer across diverse domains.
All models struggled universally with Web Search tasks, showing success rates below 30%, attributed to the dual challenge of tool selection and precise query formulation.

Error analysis revealed common failure modes including parameter errors, inaccurate tool invocation, tool omission, faulty reasoning, redundant tool invocation, and failures in information synthesis and integration.
solution: Development of MCP-RADAR, a benchmark with 507 instances across six real-world domains: Mathematical Reasoning, Web Search, Email, Calendar, File Management, and Terminal Operations, categorized into "Precise Answer" and "Fuzzy Match" tasks.

Establishment of a high-fidelity evaluation environment using a combination of authentic, verified MCP tools and meticulously replicated mock tools that mirror real-world application interfaces and parameter structures.

Implementation of a novel multi-dimensional evaluation framework utilizing objective and quantifiable metrics including Result Accuracy (RA), Dialogue Turn Success Rate (DTSR), and Computational Resource Efficiency (CRE) like token consumption.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Xi’an Jiaotong University and UMass Amherst introduce MCP-RADAR, the first multi-dimensional benchmark for evaluating Large Language Model tool-use capabilities under the industry-standard Model Context Protocol. The benchmark reveals that while LLMs can invoke tools, they struggle with semantic understanding, robust planning, and effective information synthesis, particularly in complex multi-step scenarios.

title: MCP-RADAR: A Multi-Dimensional Benchmark for Evaluating Tool Use Capabilities in Large Language Models

abstract: Large models are increasingly becoming autonomous agents that interact with real-world environments and use external tools to augment their static capabilities. However, most recent progress has focused on text-only large language models, which are limited to a single modality and therefore have narrower application scenarios. On the other hand, multimodal large models, while offering stronger perceptual capabilities, remain limited to static knowledge and lack the ability to access and leverage up-to-date web information. In this paper, we propose VSearcher, turning static multimodal model into multimodal search agent capable of long-horizon, multi-turn tool use in real-world web environments, including text search, image search, and web browsing, via reinforcement learning. Specifically, we introduce Iterative Injection Data Synthesis pipeline to generate large-scale, complex multimodal QA questions, which are further filtered with comprehensive metrics to ensure high quality and sufficient difficulty. We then adopt an SFT-then-RL training pipeline to turn base multimodal models to agent capable of multi-turn tool calling in real-world web environments. Besides, we propose a multimodal search benchmark MM-SearchExam dedicated to evaluating search capabilities of multimodal search agents, which proves highly challenging for recent proprietary models. Extensive evaluations across multiple multimodal search benchmarks reveal effectiveness of our method. VSearcher achieves superior performance compared to recent multimodal search agents and even surpasses several proprietary models on multimodal web search tasks.
title: VSearcher: Long-Horizon Multimodal Search Agent via Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based agents increasingly rely on tool use to complete real-world tasks. While existing works evaluate the LLMs' tool use capability, they largely focus on the final answers yet overlook the detailed tool usage trajectory, i.e., whether tools are selected, parameterized, and ordered correctly. We introduce TRAJECT-Bench, a trajectory-aware benchmark to comprehensively evaluate LLMs' tool use capability through diverse tasks with fine-grained evaluation metrics. TRAJECT-Bench pairs high-fidelity, executable tools across practical domains with tasks grounded in production-style APIs, and synthesizes trajectories that vary in breadth (parallel calls) and depth (interdependent chains). Besides final accuracy, TRAJECT-Bench also reports trajectory-level diagnostics, including tool selection and argument correctness, and dependency/order satisfaction. Analyses reveal failure modes such as similar tool confusion and parameter-blind selection, and scaling behavior with tool diversity and trajectory length where the bottleneck of transiting from short to mid-length trajectories is revealed, offering actionable guidance for LLMs' tool use.

keyInsights: LLMs face substantial challenges in interpreting complex user intents from indirect queries and in scaling their tool-use capabilities for longer, more numerous tool sequences.
Current retrieval-augmented generation methods for tool selection often prove insufficient for complex, implicit queries, struggling to retrieve necessary tools.

Agentic frameworks like ReAct and dynamic tool retrieval modestly improve tool-use performance, yet fundamental issues in precise tool selection and parameterization persist across state-of-the-art models.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for LLM tool use lack diverse and complex tool-use trajectories, often focusing on short or simulated interactions.
Evaluation queries in current benchmarks are typically straightforward, failing to assess LLMs' ability to infer intent from naturalistic, indirect language.
Prior evaluation schemes over-rely on final-answer metrics, which obscure specific tool selection, parameterization, and sequencing errors, hindering diagnostic analysis.
results: LLM performance shows a substantial drop on "hard" (indirect) queries compared to "simple" ones (e.g., Claude-4 Exact Match dropped from 0.846 to 0.445).
Tool-use performance degrades consistently as the number of tools in a trajectory increases, with a notable bottleneck observed when scaling from 3 to 5 tools.
The ReAct framework consistently improved Exact Match (e.g., Claude-4 on parallel hard queries from 0.445 to 0.463), with dynamic retrieval further boosting it (to 0.473 for Claude-4).
solution: Introduces TRAJECT-Bench, a benchmark featuring over 1,000 high-fidelity, executable tools from diverse real-world domains.
Synthesizes complex tool-use trajectories, including parallel and sequential structures, varying from 3 to 10+ tools.
Provides dual-difficulty user queries (simple and hard) for each trajectory, coupled with fine-grained, trajectory-aware evaluation metrics like Exact Match and Tool Usage.
paper_summary.summary: A new benchmark called TRAJECT-Bench introduces a rigorous framework for evaluating Large Language Model (LLM) agents' tool-use capabilities using over 1,000 real-world APIs, complex multi-step trajectories, and both direct and indirect user queries. Evaluations reveal LLMs struggle significantly with inferring intent from indirect queries and scaling tool use, though agentic frameworks like ReAct provide modest improvements.

title: TRAJECT-Bench:A Trajectory-Aware Benchmark for Evaluating Agentic Tool Use

abstract: As Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) advance, multimodal agents show promise in real-world tasks like web navigation and embodied intelligence. However, due to limitations in a lack of external feedback, these agents struggle with self-correction and generalization. A promising approach is to use reward models as external feedback, but there is no clear on how to select reward models for agents. Thus, there is an urgent need to build a reward bench targeted at agents. To address these challenges, we propose Agent-RewardBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate reward modeling ability in MLLMs. The benchmark is characterized by three key features: (1) Multiple dimensions and real-world agent scenarios evaluation. It covers perception, planning, and safety with 7 scenarios; (2) Step-level reward evaluation. It allows for the assessment of agent capabilities at the individual steps of a task, providing a more granular view of performance during the planning process; and (3) Appropriately difficulty and high-quality. We carefully sample from 10 diverse models, difficulty control to maintain task challenges, and manual verification to ensure the integrity of the data. Experiments demonstrate that even state-of-the-art multimodal models show limited performance, highlighting the need for specialized training in agent reward modeling. Code is available at github.

keyInsights: Agent-RewardBench proves challenging for even state-of-the-art black-box models, with Gemini-1.5-pro achieving the highest overall accuracy at 61.6%, underscoring significant room for improvement.

Current MLLMs exhibit a critical weakness in safety reward modeling, with top models scoring as low as 22.4% to 39.2% in safety evaluations, indicating a lack of targeted training in this crucial area.

A strong positive Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.981 was found between Agent-RewardBench accuracy and actual agent performance in downstream tasks (e.g., A* search), empirically validating the benchmark's relevance.

Performance generally correlates positively with model size, suggesting that larger models have a greater capacity for complex multimodal understanding and planning logic required for effective reward modeling.
originalProblem: Current MLLM agents struggle with self-correction and generalization due primarily to limitations of imitation learning and a lack of external feedback mechanisms.
There is no dedicated, unified benchmark to evaluate the *reward modeling ability* of MLLMs specifically for intelligent agents in real-world, multimodal scenarios.
Existing reward model benchmarks do not capture the unique challenges of agentic behavior, such as multi-step planning, multimodal perception, and safety considerations in complex environments.
results: Gemini-1.5-pro achieved the highest overall accuracy on the benchmark at 61.6%, closely followed by GPT-4o at 61.4%, indicating that current state-of-the-art models are far from ideal agent reward models.
Stronger overall models did not translate to better safety reward modeling; GPT-4o scored 39.2%, Claude-3.5-Sonnet scored 22.4%, and Gemini-1.5-pro scored 37.7% in safety-related evaluations.

Open-source models, particularly those at the 7B and 11B scales, demonstrated considerably lower performance compared to proprietary models, highlighting a significant gap in their agent reward modeling capabilities.
solution: Introduced Agent-RewardBench, a novel benchmark evaluating MLLMs' reward modeling capabilities across perception, planning, and safety dimensions in seven diverse real-world agent scenarios.
Implemented step-level reward evaluation, collecting positive and negative samples for each intermediate action to provide granular feedback during sequential task execution.
Ensured high-quality, appropriately difficult data through a two-stage filtering process using smaller models and rigorous manual verification by AI graduate students.
paper_summary.summary: Agent-RewardBench is introduced as the first unified benchmark to evaluate Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) as reward models for real-world agent tasks, covering perception, planning, and safety. The benchmark reveals that current MLLMs, even state-of-the-art ones, struggle significantly with safety-related reward modeling, achieving only 22.4% to 39.2% accuracy in this dimension, while demonstrating a strong correlation (Pearson 0.981) between benchmark performance and downstream agent task effectiveness.

title: Agent-RewardBench: Towards a Unified Benchmark for Reward Modeling across Perception, Planning, and Safety in Real-World Multimodal Agents

abstract:
As multimodal large language models (MLLMs) advance, MLLM-based virtual
agents have demonstrated remarkable performance. However, existing benchmarks
face significant limitations, including uncontrollable task complexity,
extensive manual annotation with limited scenarios, and a lack of
multidimensional evaluation. In response to these challenges, we introduce
OmniBench, a self-generating, cross-platform, graph-based benchmark with an
automated pipeline for synthesizing tasks of controllable complexity through
subtask composition. To evaluate the diverse capabilities of virtual agents on
the graph, we further present OmniEval, a multidimensional evaluation framework
that includes subtask-level evaluation, graph-based metrics, and comprehensive
tests across 10 capabilities. Our synthesized dataset contains 36k
graph-structured tasks across 20 scenarios, achieving a 91\% human acceptance
rate. Training on our graph-structured data shows that it can more efficiently
guide agents compared to manually annotated data. We conduct multidimensional
evaluations for various open-source and closed-source models, revealing their
performance across various capabilities and paving the way for future
advancements. Our project is available at this https URL

keyInsights: Representing tasks as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) of subtasks enables both systematic complexity control during generation and fine-grained, intermediate-step evaluation.
Automated synthesis of high-quality tasks and their corresponding evaluation functions is achievable through iterative cross-verification and semantic consistency validation.

Beyond simple success rates, multi-dimensional metrics tied to graph structure (e.g., Coverage Rate, Logical Consistency) and specific capabilities provide more actionable diagnostic insights into agent performance.
originalProblem: Existing trajectory-based benchmarks are inaccurate due to multiple valid execution paths, while result-based benchmarks lack fine-grained insights into intermediate steps.
Current benchmarks have uncontrollable and fixed task complexity, making it difficult to systematically test specific agent capabilities or adapt to evolving agent performance.

The reliance on extensive manual labor for task creation limits scalability and diversity, and existing methods lack multidimensional evaluation, aggregating performance into single, undiagnostic metrics.
results: OmniEval's metrics (Coverage Rate, Logical Consistency) exhibit strong correlation with human evaluations (CR: 0.90, LC: 0.88), validating the framework's accuracy.

Current state-of-the-art agents, including GPT-4o, show significant limitations in Subtask Identification (30.6%) and Long Instruction Following (32.2%), and perform substantially worse on graph-structured tasks (GPT-4o 20.5%) compared to linear ones.

Training agents with OmniBench's synthetic, graph-structured data improves performance on external benchmarks (e.g., AndroidControl SR by up to 0.46 points) and significantly reduces instruction sensitivity (e.g., Omni-Aguvis reduced sensitivity by 10.23 points).
solution: OmniBench is a self-generating pipeline that synthesizes graph-structured tasks, defining five fundamental complexity dimensions (Dependency, Instruction, Knowledge, Hierarchy, Branch) for controllable difficulty.

It automatically generates subtask trajectories and evaluation functions through iterative cross-verification, then composes these into coherent task graphs guided by task intents and validated for semantic consistency.

OmniEval provides a graph-based, multi-metric evaluation framework featuring novel metrics like Coverage Rate and Logical Consistency, and defines 10 fine-grained agent capabilities by combining complexity dimensions for diagnostic assessment.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Zhejiang University and Ant Group introduce OmniBench, a scalable, self-generating benchmark with graph-structured tasks, and OmniEval, a multidimensional evaluation framework for multimodal large language model (MLLM)-based virtual agents. The framework facilitates generating 36,000 diverse tasks and reveals that current MLLM agents struggle with instruction understanding and non-linear task dependencies, while demonstrating improved agent robustness when fine-tuned with OmniBench's data.

title: What Limits Virtual Agent Application? OmniBench: A Scalable  Multi-Dimensional Benchmark for Essential Virtual Agent Capabilities

abstract: Interleaved text-and-image generation represents a significant frontier for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), offering a more intuitive way to convey complex information. Current paradigms rely on either image generation or retrieval augmentation, yet they typically treat the two as mutually exclusive paths, failing to unify factuality with creativity. We argue that the next milestone in this field is Agentic Tool Planning, where the model serves as a central controller that autonomously determines when, where, and which tools to invoke to produce interleaved responses for visual-critical queries. To systematically evaluate this paradigm, we introduce ATP-Bench, a novel benchmark comprising 7,702 QA pairs (including 1,592 VQA pairs) across eight categories and 25 visual-critical intents, featuring human-verified queries and ground truths. Furthermore, to evaluate agentic planning independent of end-to-end execution and changing tool backends, we propose a Multi-Agent MLLM-as-a-Judge (MAM) system. MAM evaluates tool-call precision, identifies missed opportunities for tool use, and assesses overall response quality without requiring ground-truth references. Our extensive experiments on 10 state-of-the-art MLLMs reveal that models struggle with coherent interleaved planning and exhibit significant variations in tool-use behavior, highlighting substantial room for improvement and providing actionable guidance for advancing interleaved generation. Dataset and code are available at this https URL.
title: ATP-Bench: Towards Agentic Tool Planning for MLLM Interleaved Generation

abstract: Real-world multi-modal problems are rarely solved by a single machine learning model, and often require multi-step computational plans that involve stitching several models. Tool-augmented LLMs hold tremendous promise for automating the generation of such computational plans. However, the lack of standardized benchmarks for evaluating LLMs as planners for multi-step multi-modal tasks has prevented a systematic study of planner design decisions. Should LLMs generate a full plan in a single shot or step-by-step? Should they invoke tools directly with Python code or through structured data formats like JSON? Does feedback improve planning? To answer these questions and more, we introduce m&m's: a benchmark containing 4K+ multi-step multi-modal tasks involving 33 tools that include multi-modal models, (free) public APIs, and image processing modules. For each of these task queries, we provide automatically generated plans using this realistic toolset. We further provide a high-quality subset of 1,565 task plans that are human-verified and correctly executable. With m&m's, we evaluate 10 popular LLMs with 2 planning strategies (multi-step vs. step-by-step planning), 2 plan formats (JSON vs. code), and 3 types of feedback (parsing/verification/execution). Finally, we summarize takeaways from our extensive experiments. Our dataset and code are available on HuggingFace (this https URL) and Github (this https URL).

keyInsights: Multi-step planning, where LLMs generate a complete plan upfront, consistently yields better tool selection and higher plan executability compared to step-by-step planning.
Incorporating external feedback, particularly verification and execution feedback, significantly improves LLMs' ability to predict correct argument names and produce executable plans.
Structured JSON-format plan generation results in substantially higher pass rates (executability) across LLMs, even when tool selection performance is comparable to Python code generation.
originalProblem: Intelligent agents require the decomposition of complex, real-world problems into multiple subtasks, each needing specialized multi-modal tool invocations.
A lack of standardized, realistic benchmarks prevents systematic evaluation of LLMs acting as planners for intricate multi-step, multi-modal tasks.
Existing tool-use benchmarks often lack realistic execution environments, either omitting actual tool implementations, emulating tool behavior with LLMs, or relying on placeholder inputs.
results: Multi-step planning improved the pass rate by over 10% for smaller models, with GPT-3.5 showing a 21.8% increase in performance compared to step-by-step planning.
Verification and execution feedback increased Argname-F1 by 1-4% and pass rates by up to 20-30% for most models, although it sometimes caused a slight decrease (less than 5%) in Tool-F1.
JSON-format plan generation consistently achieved higher pass rates than Python code generation across all tested models, indicating greater plan executability.
solution: The m&m’s benchmark is introduced, comprising over 4,000 multi-step multi-modal tasks, including 1,565 human-verified executable plans utilizing a curated set of 33 real-world tools.

The benchmark’s creation involves sampling valid tool sequences, pairing them with real input examples, generating realistic user queries, and creating ground truth plans, all subjected to human verification.

A modular planning agent system, based on AutoGen, supports evaluating different planning strategies (multi-step vs. step-by-step), plan formats (JSON vs. Python code), and feedback mechanisms (parsing, verification, execution).
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduce m&m’s, a benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) as planning agents for multi-step multi-modal tasks, using over 1,500 human-verified tasks with actual tool implementations and real inputs. Experiments reveal that multi-step planning, JSON plan format, and external feedback mechanisms generally improve the performance of LLM planning agents.

title: m&m's: A Benchmark to Evaluate Tool-Use for multi-step multi-modal Tasks

abstract: Recent advances in Multi-Modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have enabled unified processing of language, vision, and structured inputs, opening the door to complex tasks such as logical deduction, spatial reasoning, and scientific analysis. Despite their promise, the reasoning capabilities of MLLMs, particularly those augmented with intermediate thinking traces (MLLMs-T), remain poorly understood and lack standardized evaluation benchmarks. Existing work focuses primarily on perception or final answer correctness, offering limited insight into how models reason or fail across modalities. To address this gap, we introduce the MMLU-Reason, a new benchmark designed to rigorously evaluate multi-modal reasoning with explicit thinking. The MMLU-Reason comprises 1) a high-difficulty dataset of 1,083 questions spanning six diverse reasoning types with symbolic depth and multi-hop demands and 2) a modular Reasoning Trace Evaluation Pipeline (RTEP) for assessing reasoning quality beyond accuracy through metrics like relevance, consistency, and structured error annotations. Empirical results show that MLLMs-T overall outperform non-thinking counterparts, but even top models like Claude-3.7-Sonnet and Gemini-2.5 Pro suffer from reasoning pathologies such as inconsistency and overthinking. This benchmark reveals persistent gaps between accuracy and reasoning quality and provides an actionable evaluation pipeline for future model development. Overall, the MMLU-Reason offers a scalable foundation for evaluating, comparing, and improving the next generation of multi-modal reasoning systems.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

title: MMLU-Reason: Benchmarking Multi-Task Multi-modal Language Understanding and Reasoning

abstract: With the increasing demand for step-wise, cross-modal, and knowledge-grounded reasoning, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are evolving beyond the traditional fixed retrieve-then-generate paradigm toward more sophisticated agentic multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (MM-RAG). Existing benchmarks, however, mainly focus on simplified QA with short retrieval chains, leaving adaptive planning and multimodal reasoning underexplored. We present MC-Search, the first benchmark for agentic MM-RAG with long, step-wise annotated reasoning chains spanning five representative reasoning structures. Each example specifies sub-questions, retrieval modalities, supporting facts, and intermediate answers, with fidelity ensured by HAVE (Hop-wise Attribution and Verification of Evidence), resulting in 3,333 high-quality examples averaging 3.7 hops. Beyond answer accuracy, MC-Search introduces new process-level metrics for reasoning quality, stepwise retrieval and planning accuracy. By developing a unified agentic MM-RAG pipeline, we benchmark six leading MLLMs and reveal systematic issues such as over- and under-retrieval and modality-misaligned planning. Finally, we introduce Search-Align, a process-supervised fine-tuning framework leveraging verified reasoning chains, showing that our data not only enables faithful evaluation but also improves planning and retrieval fidelity in open-source MLLMs.

keyInsights: Open-source MLLMs, while initially weaker, show substantial improvements in agentic MM-RAG capabilities when fine-tuned with process-level supervision from SEARCH-ALIGN.
Performance consistently degrades as reasoning chain length increases, with complex multimodal topologies like Parallel Image-Text Fork and Multi-Images Fork posing the greatest challenges.
Models exhibit a strong bias towards textual retrieval, resulting in significantly lower image retrieval coverage without explicit image inputs, indicating a major issue in modality-aware planning.
originalProblem: Existing MM-RAG benchmarks primarily focus on simplified question-answering formats with short, fixed retrieval chains, failing to assess adaptive, multi-step reasoning.
Current evaluation frameworks lack fine-grained, step-wise annotations and explicit reasoning topologies, making it difficult to diagnose specific weaknesses in MLLMs beyond final answer accuracy.
MLLMs struggle with effective multimodal evidence integration and adaptive retrieval strategies necessary for complex, long-horizon reasoning queries.
results: SEARCH-ALIGN significantly improved open-source models; Qwen2.5-VL-7B achieved average gains of +13.7 F1 and +16.0 HPS, while InternVL3.5-8B saw +2.8 F1 and +12.0 HPS.
All evaluated models demonstrated a consistent drop in performance for reasoning chains longer than 3 hops, highlighting the difficulty of sustained long-horizon reasoning.

Error analysis revealed Retrieval-Failure (84.7%), Hallucinated-Entity/Attribute (75.8%), and Step-Omission (74.3%) as dominant error types, alongside Modality-Mismatch (33.46%) indicating planning instability.
solution: Introduced MC-SEARCH, a benchmark featuring five distinct, structured multimodal reasoning topologies and step-wise annotated golden reasoning chains, constructed from Wikipedia knowledge.

Developed HAVE (Hop-wise Attribution and Verification of Evidence), a robust filtering mechanism that ensures each step in the reasoning chains is necessary and non-redundant, addressing hallucination and redundancy.
Proposed SEARCH-ALIGN, a process-supervised fine-tuning framework that leverages these verified reasoning chains to improve the planning and retrieval fidelity of open-source MLLMs.
paper_summary.summary: The MC-SEARCH benchmark and SEARCH-ALIGN framework were developed to evaluate and enhance agentic multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (MM-RAG) systems with long, structured reasoning chains. This work demonstrated that process-level supervision significantly improves open-source multimodal large language models (MLLMs) in complex, multi-hop reasoning tasks, showing gains like +13.7 F1 and +16.0 HPS for Qwen2.5-VL-7B.

title: MC-Search: Evaluating and Enhancing Multimodal Agentic Search with Structured Long Reasoning Chains

abstract:
Significant focus has been placed on integrating large language models (LLMs)
with various tools in developing general-purpose agents. This poses a challenge
to LLMs' tool-use capabilities. However, there are evident gaps between
existing tool-use evaluations and real-world scenarios. Current evaluations
often use AI-generated queries, single-step tasks, dummy tools, and text-only
interactions, failing to reveal the agents' real-world problem-solving
abilities effectively. To address this, we propose GTA, a benchmark for General
Tool Agents, featuring three main aspects: (i) Real user queries: human-written
queries with simple real-world objectives but implicit tool-use, requiring the
LLM to reason the suitable tools and plan the solution steps. (ii) Real
deployed tools: an evaluation platform equipped with tools across perception,
operation, logic, and creativity categories to evaluate the agents' actual task
execution performance. (iii) Real multimodal inputs: authentic image files,
such as spatial scenes, web page screenshots, tables, code snippets, and
printed/handwritten materials, used as the query contexts to align with
real-world scenarios closely. We design 229 real-world tasks and executable
tool chains to evaluate mainstream LLMs. Our findings show that real-world user
queries are challenging for existing LLMs, with GPT-4 completing less than 50%
of the tasks and most LLMs achieving below 25%. This evaluation reveals the
bottlenecks in the tool-use capabilities of current LLMs in real-world
scenarios, which provides future direction for advancing general-purpose tool
agents. The code and dataset are available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Argument prediction is a primary bottleneck for current LLMs, with models struggling to correctly format and provide necessary arguments for tool invocation, limiting overall performance.
Different LLM series exhibit distinct tool invocation behaviors: some are aggressive (frequent but error-prone), others conservative (less frequent but more accurate), and some neutral (balanced).
Fine-tuning helps improve instruction-following and tool selection, but it does not adequately resolve the challenges in argument prediction accuracy.
originalProblem: Existing tool-use benchmarks rely on AI-generated queries, which are often simplistic and hint at tool-use, failing to test complex reasoning and planning.
Current evaluations frequently use virtual or simulated tools, preventing a true end-to-end assessment of an agent's ability to execute tasks reliably with real-world complexities.
Benchmarks predominantly focus on text-only interactions, neglecting the multimodal nature of real-world problems that require processing visual information like images or tables.
results: Evaluated mainstream LLMs, including advanced models like GPT-4 and GPT-4o, completed fewer than 50% of tasks, indicating significant challenges in real-world tool-use scenarios.
API-based models consistently outperformed open-source models across metrics, with GPT-4 achieving an AnsAcc of 46.59% and GPT-4o 41.52%, suggesting more robust capabilities.

Larger models generally performed better within their series, but performance varied across different model architectures and fine-tuning strategies, indicating that size alone is not the sole determinant of success.
solution: The GTA (General Tool Agents) benchmark was developed using human-written natural language queries based on authentic multimodal inputs (image files).
It integrates 14 real, executable tools across perception, operation, logic, and creativity categories, allowing for end-to-end task accomplishment evaluation.
A rigorous manual process was used to construct ground truth executable tool chains for each query, ensuring precision and reliability for evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University and Shanghai AI Laboratory propose GTA, a benchmark for evaluating LLM-based agents that addresses limitations of existing tool-use evaluations by incorporating human-written queries, real deployed tools, and multimodal inputs. The evaluation of 16 mainstream LLMs revealed that current models struggle with real-world tool-use, achieving overall low accuracy (e.g., GPT-4 at 46.59% Answer Accuracy), with argument prediction identified as the primary bottleneck for performance.

title: GTA: A Benchmark for General Tool Agents

abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have broadened the scope of AI applications. Existing automatic evaluation methodologies for MLLMs are mainly limited in evaluating queries without considering user experiences, inadequately addressing the nuances of creative and associative multimodal tasks. However, the open-ended and subjective nature of such tasks poses a significant challenge to the evaluation methodology, where it is difficult to define the ground-truth answers for them. To this end, in our paper, we propose a new evaluation paradigm for MLLMs, which is evaluating MLLMs with per-sample criteria using potent MLLM as the judge. To validate the feasibility and effectiveness of this paradigm, we design a benchmark, dubbed MLLM-Bench, by curating the evaluation samples across six comprehensive cognitive levels. We benchmark 21 popular MLLMs in a pairwise-comparison fashion, showing diverse performance across models. Moreover, the validity of our benchmark manifests itself in reaching 88.02% agreement with human evaluation. We contend that the proposed paradigm explores the potential of MLLMs as effective evaluation tools with the help of per-sample criteria. See online leaderboard at \url{this https URL}.

organization_info.name: Shenzhen Research Institute of Big Data
The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

keyInsights: Vision-capable MLLMs, when guided by specific per-sample criteria, can serve as reliable and scalable automated judges for evaluating other MLLMs, demonstrating strong alignment with human judgment.
Per-sample criteria are essential for accurately evaluating open-ended MLLM responses, significantly improving assessment quality and human-judge agreement.

A structured taxonomy of capabilities, like the adapted Bloom's Taxonomy in MLLM-Bench, provides a holistic framework for understanding and comparing MLLM strengths and weaknesses across cognitive levels.
originalProblem: Existing MLLM evaluation benchmarks primarily use close-ended queries, failing to capture nuanced performance on open-ended, subjective tasks like creativity or ethical judgment.
Human evaluation, while ideal for subjective tasks, is costly and inefficient for large-scale, continuous assessment of MLLMs.
Prior attempts using pure Large Language Models (LLMs) as evaluators are limited by their inability to directly perceive visual contexts in multimodal scenarios.
results: Proprietary MLLMs such as Claude-3 (0.83 win rate) and GPT-4V (0.80 win rate) achieved the highest performance in pairwise comparisons against LLaVA-v1.5-13B.
GPT-4V's judgments, when utilizing per-sample criteria, exhibited an 88.02% agreement with aggregated human evaluations, validating its effectiveness as an automated judge.
The per-sample criteria improved agreement with human evaluation by over 5% compared to GPT-4V without criteria, highlighting their importance for nuanced assessment.
The MLLM-Bench evaluation framework demonstrated high reproducibility and robustness, with alternative MLLM judges showing strong correlation (Spearman correlation of 0.95 with GPT-4V's rankings).
solution: Proposed an "MLLM-as-the-Judge" paradigm, where a powerful MLLM (e.g., GPT-4V) evaluates other MLLMs using flexible "per-sample criteria" instead of fixed ground-truth answers.
Developed MLLM-Bench, a comprehensive dataset of 420 image-instruction pairs spanning 42 distinct MLLM capabilities categorized by an adapted Bloom's Taxonomy.
The dataset creation involved a multi-stage annotation pipeline, including GPT-4V assisted instruction crafting and annotation of 1-3 specific evaluation criteria for each sample.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shenzhen Research Institute of Big Data and The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen developed MLLM-Bench, an evaluation framework that employs a potent Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) as a judge, guided by per-sample criteria, to assess MLLMs on open-ended tasks. This framework achieved 88.02% agreement with human judgments and revealed diverse performance across 21 MLLMs, with top proprietary models like Claude-3 achieving an 0.83 win rate.

title: MLLM-Bench: Evaluating Multimodal LLMs with Per-sample Criteria

abstract:
Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have increased the demand for
comprehensive benchmarks to evaluate their capabilities as human-like agents.
Existing benchmarks, while useful, often focus on specific application
scenarios, emphasizing task completion but failing to dissect the underlying
skills that drive these outcomes. This lack of granularity makes it difficult
to deeply discern where failures stem from. Additionally, setting up these
environments requires considerable effort, and issues of unreliability and
reproducibility sometimes arise, especially in interactive tasks. To address
these limitations, we introduce the Massive Multitask Agent Understanding
(MMAU) benchmark, featuring comprehensive offline tasks that eliminate the need
for complex environment setups. It evaluates models across five domains,
including Tool-use, Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) QA, Data Science and Machine
Learning coding, Contest-level programming and Mathematics, and covers five
essential capabilities: Understanding, Reasoning, Planning, Problem-solving,
and Self-correction. With a total of 20 meticulously designed tasks
encompassing over 3K distinct prompts, MMAU provides a comprehensive framework
for evaluating the strengths and limitations of LLM agents. By testing 18
representative models on MMAU, we provide deep and insightful analyses.
Ultimately, MMAU not only sheds light on the capabilities and limitations of
LLM agents but also enhances the interpretability of their performance.
Datasets and evaluation scripts of MMAU are released at
this https URL
title: MMAU: A Holistic Benchmark of Agent Capabilities Across Diverse Domains

abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based agents are increasingly used to solve complex tasks involving tool use, such as web browsing, code execution, and data analysis. However, current evaluation benchmarks do not adequately assess their ability to solve real-world tasks that require synthesizing information from multiple sources and inferring insights beyond simple fact retrieval. To address this, we introduce DEEPSYNTH, a novel benchmark designed to evaluate agents on realistic, time-consuming problems that combine information gathering, synthesis, and structured reasoning to produce insights. DEEPSYNTH contains 120 tasks collected across 7 domains and data sources covering 67 countries. DEEPSYNTH is constructed using a multi-stage data collection pipeline that requires annotators to collect official data sources, create hypotheses, perform manual analysis, and design tasks with verifiable answers. When evaluated on DEEPSYNTH, 11 state-of-the-art LLMs and deep research agents achieve a maximum F1 score of 8.97 and 17.5 on the LLM-judge metric, underscoring the difficulty of the benchmark. Our analysis reveals that current agents struggle with hallucinations and reasoning over large information spaces, highlighting DEEPSYNTH as a crucial benchmark for guiding future research.
